title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: This PR ships two useful endpoints but has significant problems across every review dimension. Security: the customer-history response actively serialises a `card_number` field — a hard PCI-DSS block. Performance: the history controller loads the entire transaction table into memory to filter in JS, then issues one full refund-store scan per transaction (N+1 × M), with no pagination. State management: `RefundsService` hides a module-level mutable store that is shared across every test run and cannot be injected or reset. Correctness: the sort compares `created_at` against a domain object that has `createdAt`, so every response is silently unsorted; a missing transaction returns `200 {}` instead of `404`. Tests: all three assertions (`toBeDefined`, `toBeDefined`, `toBeLessThan(500)`) pass regardless of whether the feature works. The PR should not merge until at minimum the PAN leak, the N+1 pattern, the singleton store, the sort bug, and the wrong status code are fixed.

findings.title: card_number returned in API response — PCI-DSS hard block
findings.message: Line 43 deliberately casts `tx` through `as unknown as { card_number?: string }` to fish out a `card_number` field and forward it in the response. PCI-DSS prohibits returning a PAN from any API endpoint, full stop. Even if the in-memory store never populates this field today, the code is written to expose it the moment it appears on the object. The `Transaction` domain type already carries `cardLast4` — that is all that should ever cross the wire. Remove the `card_number` key from the response entirely, and rotate any environment where this code has already run against real card data.

findings.title: console.log of transaction IDs bypasses pino redaction allowlist
findings.message: Lines 25 and 55 use `console.log` to emit `transactionId`, `refundId`, and `amount` directly to stdout. The project ships a pino logger with a field-level redaction allowlist specifically to prevent sensitive payment data from leaking into log pipelines — `console.log` completely bypasses that control. PCI-DSS requires that transaction data flow through controlled, auditable logging. Replace both calls with the injected logger: `logger.info({ refundId: refund.refundId, transactionId: tx.id }, 'refund created')`. The same issue is present in `customerHistoryController.ts` line 15.

findings.title: Load-all-then-filter in memory — O(total transactions) per request
findings.message: Lines 18–25 call `this.transactions.findAll()` to pull every transaction in the store into JS memory, then iterate and filter by `customerId`. This is fast against the 10-row seed fixture and a full-table scan on 5 M rows. The PR even adds `findAll()` to the `TransactionRepository` interface solely to enable this pattern — that's a tell. Add a `findByCustomer(customerId: string): Promise<Transaction[]>` method to the repository interface and implement it with a targeted lookup (for the in-memory impl, an index Map keyed by customerId). Push the predicate to the store; count of queries per request should be O(1), not O(all customers).

findings.title: N+1 × M: one full refund-store scan per transaction in the result set
findings.message: The loop on lines 36–37 calls `enrichTransactionWithRefunds(tx)` for each of the customer's N transactions. That method calls `listRefundsForTransaction`, which iterates `Object.keys(refundsStore)` — O(M total refunds) — for every single call. For a customer with N transactions and M total refunds in the system this is O(N × M) work per page. Batch it: add a `findByTransactionIds(ids: string[])` method, fetch all matching refunds in a single pass, then group them in memory with a `Map<transactionId, RefundResult[]>` before attaching. This collapses N × M to N + M.

findings.title: No pagination — customer history endpoint is unbounded
findings.message: The endpoint loads and returns every transaction for a customer with no `limit`, `offset`, or cursor. A customer with thousands of transactions returns everything in a single response buffered in memory. Add a `limit` (default 50) and a cursor or offset to both the query string and the response envelope before this endpoint sees production traffic. Every list endpoint needs a page size; unbounded reads are a latent OOM.

findings.title: Module-level mutable singleton `refundsStore` — hidden global state
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` is a module-scoped mutable variable shared by every instance of `RefundsService` in the process. The rest of the codebase injects repositories via constructors (`TransactionRepository` flows through `PaymentsService`, `RefundsService`, and `CustomerHistoryController`) — this breaks that pattern in the one place it matters most. Concrete consequences: (1) tests cannot reset it between runs — refunds created in test case 1 are visible when test case 3 runs, because `createApp()` creates a fresh `transactionRepo` but the module-level store is never cleared; (2) you can never have two independent `RefundsService` instances; (3) it can't be swapped for a fake in unit tests. Create a `RefundRepository` interface, inject it via the constructor, and provide an `InMemoryRefundRepository` exactly as the transaction store is handled.

findings.title: `as unknown as` double-cast silences the compiler over a design problem
findings.message: The expression `(tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number` is a double-cast escape hatch. The compiler is telling you `card_number` doesn't exist on `Transaction` — the right response is to listen to it, not to shut it up. The domain type already has `cardLast4`; there is no legitimate reason to reach for `card_number` here (and returning it is a PCI violation — see the security finding). Delete the cast and the field.

findings.title: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but never used
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is defined and exported but never imported or applied anywhere in the diff. The customer-history controller reads `req.params.id` directly, bypassing it. Either wire it up or delete it — dead exports are future confusion and imply validation that isn't happening.

findings.title: Returns `200 {}` when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: When `this.refunds.refund()` returns `null` because the transaction doesn't exist, the controller responds with `res.status(200).json({})`. The comment even says "transaction didn't exist" — yet the code sends a success status. An empty-body `200` is indistinguishable from a successful no-op refund to any caller, monitoring tool, or retry client. Return `res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' })` so the contract is honest.

findings.title: Sort key `created_at` doesn't exist on Transaction — sort is always a no-op
findings.message: The comparator on lines 28–32 references `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but the `Transaction` domain type uses camelCase `createdAt`. The `(a: any, b: any)` cast hides this from the compiler. At runtime, `a.created_at` is `undefined` for every element; `undefined < undefined` is always `false`, so the comparator returns `0` for every pair and the sort is a complete no-op. Transactions are returned in arbitrary insertion order, not newest-first as the comment and PR description claim. Fix to `a.createdAt` / `b.createdAt` and drop the `any` casts so the compiler protects against this in future.

findings.title: All three test assertions pass regardless of whether the feature works
findings.message:
• Line 21: `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` — passes whether the refund is correct, empty, or a 500 error body.
• Line 29: `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` — passes for 200, 404, and 422 equally. The code under test currently returns `200 {}` for a missing transaction, which is a bug this test is supposed to catch — but it doesn't.
• Line 37: `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` — always truthy.

For the success path: assert `expect(refund.status).toBe(201)` and `expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ transactionId: charge.body.id, amount: 1000, status: 'refunded' })`. For the not-found path: assert `expect(refund.status).toBe(404)`. Add tests for partial refund, over-refund, invalid amount (negative, zero), and missing `transaction_id`. These are exactly the cases that bite in prod and that happy-path tests miss.

findings.title: `console.log` instead of the project's pino logger
findings.message: Line 15 logs with `console.log` where the project uses pino. `console.log` bypasses log levels, structured fields, and the redaction allowlist. Inject the logger and use `logger.info({ customerId }, 'customer history requested')`. The same issue appears at `refundsService.ts` lines 25 and 55 — see the security finding there for the stronger concern about those.

findings.title: Weak validation: `amount` accepts zero, negative, and fractional values
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` allows `0`, `-500`, and `9.99`. The service code even has a comment acknowledging the ambiguity between cents and dollars — that ambiguity should be eliminated at the boundary, not worked around inside business logic. Apply `z.number().int().positive()` to reject non-integer and non-positive values. Also tighten `transaction_id: z.string().min(1)` to reject empty strings. Enforcing a clean contract at the edge means the service can trust its inputs.

findings.title: Over-refund silently capped to original amount — caller is not told
findings.message: When `refundAmount > tx.amount`, the code silently downgrades to `tx.amount` (or applies a speculative dollars-to-cents heuristic). The caller requested a specific amount and receives a `201 succeeded` response with a different amount and no indication their request was altered. The dollar/cents heuristic on lines 38–41 is especially dangerous: a caller sending `amount: 1000` meaning "$10.00" against a `tx.amount` of `500` (50¢) will have their refund silently changed from $10 to $5. Reject the over-refund explicitly: return a `422 Unprocessable Entity` with `{ error: 'amount exceeds original transaction' }` instead of silently capping.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` comparing strings — should be `===`
findings.message: `tx.customerId == customerId` uses loose equality where the codebase consistently uses `===`. For two string values this is harmless today, but it signals a style break and can produce unexpected coercions if either value is ever not a plain string. Use `tx.customerId === customerId`.

findings.title: Response shape mixes snake_case and camelCase inconsistently
findings.message: The response object on lines 38–50 uses snake_case for `card_number`, `card_last4`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at`, and `refund_total`, while `RefundResult` (same PR) uses camelCase `refundId`, `transactionId`, `createdAt`. The existing `/charge` and `/transactions/:id` endpoints follow camelCase. A mixed contract forces every consumer to special-case this endpoint. Pick one convention — camelCase matches the rest of the codebase — and apply it uniformly. Note that the sort bug (filed separately) is a direct consequence of mixing conventions between the domain object and the response shape.

findings.title: `_reason` parameter accepted but silently discarded
findings.message: The `_reason` parameter is accepted by the public `refund()` method signature but never stored, returned, or logged. If `reason` is not part of the data model yet, remove the parameter so the signature is honest about what it does. If it is intended to be stored (which makes sense for audit trails on financial refunds), add it to `RefundResult` and persist it.
